text:
0 according to `system.time()`, but I was thinking on this comparison: <https://jcarroll.com.au/2025/12/05/haskell-is-a-great-language-for-data-science/> . With a large enough vector it takes a while and even hung momentarily my computer and this might be relevant for sorting/ordering data.frames and matrices:
```x <- seq_len(1e9)
system.time(rev(rev(x)))
##   user  system elapsed 
##   2.63   10.25  118.80 ```

text:
```> microbenchmark(rev(rev(1:1000)))
Unit: microseconds
             expr    min    lq     mean  median      uq    max neval
 rev(rev(1:1000)) 12.216 13.04 17.07408 14.0115 20.8985 35.848   100```

elements.text:
> microbenchmark(rev(rev(1:1000)))
Unit: microseconds
             expr    min    lq     mean  median      uq    max neval
 rev(rev(1:1000)) 12.216 13.04 17.07408 14.0115 20.8985 35.848   100

text: I prefer {bench} but the time spent is not the main point. The point is not that rev(rev could "know" it needs to return the original vector without calculating anything and that could be achieved with ALTREP. Similar to the `sort(unique` and `n_distinct`  "issues": there is room for improving evaluation on base R with ALTREP.

text:
> Wonder what algo it does for reversing if it needs more than 16GB of RAm to reverse 1GB vector…
Probably because the initial vector is ALREP but using rev materializes it and then it needs to revert it.

elements.text: Wonder what algo it does for reversing if it needs more than 16GB of RAm to reverse 1GB vector…
Probably because the initial vector is ALREP but using rev materializes it and then it needs to revert it.

text:
Yes, I get it. I don’t know much about ALTREP but I’ve seen it in action when dealing with sum arithmetic sequence.
So, generally approach on calculations based on a knowledge of shape of data maybe quite powerful approach. I need to read more about ALTREP, what it currently is capable of and what can be add to it.

text: Altrep is basically capable of everything in this regard. It allows for deferred evaluation and allocation, until a copy is requested (e.g. via actually using the vector).

elements.text: Altrep is basically capable of everything in this regard. It allows for deferred evaluation and allocation, until a copy is requested (e.g. via actually using the vector).

text:
Other idea is algebraic/symbolic evaluation or some kind of execution plan. So we can just spot `rev->rev` and just return `no_operation`
There are also other areas for such evaluation. I remember Marek Gągolewski mentioning on his lecture that interesting would be to *automatically* change the execution order to acknowledge the fact that order of operation is not affecting the result at all but performance hugely.

text:
I’m doing a project currently which deepens my knowledge on R’s graphics and I hope will give me some insight and foundations to experiment with different approach to graphics generation.
So as I like very much to discuss different topics on R’s internals, you are welcome to throw anything at me. However I cannot promise you I would undertake any of it :smile:

text: There is so much to do. I have extendr as I am the maintainer, I have R specific contributions to Rust, then I have work.. And these are small, yet "unknown" things. I just want to fight the rot, and don't need to benefit from it in any way.

elements.text: There is so much to do. I have extendr as I am the maintainer, I have R specific contributions to Rust, then I have work.. And these are small, yet "unknown" things. I just want to fight the rot, and don't need to benefit from it in any way.

text: I also discovered that one can yank libtool out completely from R, and then you'll build R without so many redundant checks.. It is a bigger project, but it is doable.

elements.text: I also discovered that one can yank libtool out completely from R, and then you'll build R without so many redundant checks.. It is a bigger project, but it is doable.

text: So, if you want more of these, please hit me up. I am getting old, and these are already 4 months old discoveries.. and I won't be marshalling them myself if I know myself...

text: <@U07MWC5T8FJ> Thanks for elaborating here your reasons. It is great to hear them as I often got that feeling. At the same time if you went out of your way to prepare a patch you could perhaps go an extra mile and submit it to the official channels. You don't need to be responsive on bugzilla but (eventually) it will be picked up by other contributors and used or discussed.
